one_line_takeaway: RIPRAG shows that treating a fully opaque RAG pipeline as an RL environment -- using only a binary 'did the poisoned answer stick' signal plus a BM25-based dense similarity reward, optimized via a novel batch-level (not group-level) policy-gradient method called BRPO -- lets a small attacker-trained LM substantially outpoison prior black-box and surrogate-gradient methods, especially at low poisoning budgets (M=1) and against complex hybrid retrievers, though RAGuard-style defenses still meaningfully suppress (not eliminate) the attack.
key_finding: RIPRAG (black-box) beats both PoisonedRAG baselines across nearly all 72 cells of Table 1 (3 datasets x 4 LLMs x 2 retriever settings x 2 M values). The paper's headline number: 'up to 0.72 ASR improvement compared to baseline methods' (stated in abstract; Section 5.2 specifies this as up to 0.65 over PoisonedRAG black-box and 0.72 over PoisonedRAG fake-white-box). Concrete Table 1 examples: on NQ with GLM4-9B, naive retriever, M=1, RIPRAG reaches 0.94 ASR vs PoisonedRAG black-box 0.29 and fake-white-box 0.22; on HotpotQA with GLM4-9B naive M=1, RIPRAG reaches 1.00 vs 0.53/0.49. Under the complex (hybrid) retriever, RIPRAG maintains 0.49-1.00 ASR across datasets while PoisonedRAG deteriorates to 0.12-0.59. In low-poisoning-rate settings (M=1), RIPRAG achieves 0.35-1.00 ASR versus PoisonedRAG's 0.12-0.61. Against defenses (Table 2, InternLM2.5-7B, complex retriever, M=1): RAGuard is the strongest defense, cutting RIPRAG's ASR to 0.10-0.16 (vs. undefended 0.58-0.86), but doubling the QLoRA rank used to train the Poisoning SLM raised ASR back to 0.23-0.28 ('RAGuard*' row), showing the residual weakness is attributable to trainable-parameter capacity rather than a fundamental defense bypass limit. Ablations (Table 3, naive retriever, M=3, Qwen3-8B): removing the similarity reward causes the largest collapse (NQ 0.72->0.09, MS-MARCO 0.78->0.06, HotpotQA 0.82->0.24); replacing BRPO with standard GRPO also causes severe degradation (NQ 0.72->0.17, MS-MARCO 0.78->0.14); removing the attack reward degrades MS-MARCO most (0.78->0.20) while NQ/HotpotQA degrade less (0.72->0.48, 0.82->0.76); adding a reference model back reduces all three (e.g. NQ 0.72->0.50). Cost: RIPRAG training took about 1 GPU hour vs. about 3 GPU hours for the fake-white-box PoisonedRAG variant; training against DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp cost about $0.7 per instance in API calls.
core_contribution: RIPRAG, a black-box RAG poisoning attack framework that treats the target RAG system purely as an input-output oracle. Its core novelty is 'Reinforcement Learning from Black-box Feedback' (RLBF): a small language model (Poisoning SLM) generates candidate poisoned documents, injects them into the RAG database, observes only whether the target system's answer matches the attacker's desired answer, and uses that binary signal (plus a dense similarity/lexical-match reward) to update its policy. To make RL training stable under this sparse, adversarial setting, the authors also propose Batch Relative Policy Optimization (BRPO), which normalizes reward advantages across an entire batch of distinct queries rather than within small per-query groups (as GRPO does), avoiding vanishing-gradient collapse caused by homogeneous candidate rewards. The paper additionally claims to be the first to benchmark black-box RAG poisoning attacks against RAG systems with advanced targeted defenses (query rewriting, HyDE, RAGuard) rather than only undefended/naive pipelines.
threat_model: Attacker goal: for a target question q with attacker-chosen desired answer a_tgt, craft and inject M poisoned documents into the RAG system's database so the system's generated answer to q equals a_tgt (e.g., poisoning 'Which company does Taobao belong to?' to output 'ByteDance' instead of the correct 'Alibaba'). Attacker knowledge: strictly limited to knowing (1) the target is a RAG-based QA system and (2) it has a retrievable database -- no knowledge of retriever, reranker, LLM, or how components are wired together (explicitly contrasted with prior 'black-box' work like LIAR, which assumes a white-box retriever, or Topic-FlipRAG, which uses an open-source surrogate retriever as a gradient proxy). Attacker capabilities: exactly two actions -- inject documents into the database, and query/chat with the QA system and observe its final answer. No gradient access, no retriever scores, no internal logits.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 8): (1) RLBF requires substantial repeated interaction with the target system during training, which may be impractical against systems with rate limiting or attack-detection mechanisms; (2) the method assumes the attacker can successfully inject documents into the target database, which may not hold against systems with rigorous content moderation/ingestion controls; (3) performance depends on the quality/diversity of the initial query set used for training, potentially limiting generalization to unseen question types or domains. Additional grounded observations from the text itself: the sole black-box baseline compared is PoisonedRAG (justified as the only prior method matching their strict no-internal-knowledge threat model), so the empirical superiority claims rest on comparison to one baseline family, not a broad field; the similarity reward and BM25 usage for reward computation is explicitly borrowed from PoisonedRAG's design rather than novel; RAGuard, the one defense evaluated that meaningfully suppresses RIPRAG, was not fully defeated even with expanded model capacity (ASR only partially recovered to 0.23-0.28 versus baseline 0.58-0.86 without defense).

quantitative_results: Abstract/Section 5.2 headline: RIPRAG achieves up to 0.72 ASR improvement over baselines (specifically up to 0.65 over PoisonedRAG black-box and up to 0.72 over PoisonedRAG fake-white-box)
NQ, GLM4-9B, naive retriever, M=1: RIPRAG 0.94 vs PoisonedRAG black-box 0.29 vs PoisonedRAG fake-white-box 0.22
HotpotQA, GLM4-9B, naive retriever, M=1: RIPRAG 1.00 vs PoisonedRAG black-box 0.53 vs fake-white-box 0.49
Complex/hybrid retriever setting overall: RIPRAG maintains 0.49-1.00 ASR across datasets while PoisonedRAG falls to 0.12-0.59
Low poisoning rate (M=1) overall: RIPRAG achieves 0.35-1.00 ASR vs PoisonedRAG's 0.12-0.61

Defense evaluation (Table 2, InternLM2.5-7B, complex retriever, M=1): undefended RIPRAG ASR 0.58-0.86; RAGuard reduces RIPRAG to 0.10-0.16 ASR (PoisonedRAG likewise drops to 0.06-0.11 under RAGuard); doubling QLoRA rank ('RAGuard*') raises RIPRAG ASR to 0.23-0.28

Ablation (Table 3, naive retriever, M=3, Qwen3-8B): full RIPRAG NQ/HotpotQA/MS-MARCO = 0.72/0.82/0.78; w/o similarity reward = 0.09/0.24/0.06; w/o BRPO (i.e. using GRPO) = 0.17/0.61/0.14; w/o attack reward = 0.48/0.76/0.20; with reference model added = 0.50/0.66/0.55
Training cost: RIPRAG ~1 GPU hour vs. fake-white-box PoisonedRAG ~3 GPU hours; ~$0.7 API cost per training instance against DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp
baselines_compared: PoisonedRAG (fake white-box variant using Contriever as an open-source surrogate retriever for gradient-based optimization)
